annotations.label: Addition

anchor.snippet: Addition , usually denoted with the plus sign

annotations.note: Generic addition is the `Add`/`HAdd` typeclass used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: 3 + 2 = 5 apples

anchor.snippet: the adjacent image shows two columns of apples

annotations.note: Pictorial illustrative example, not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Commutativity of addition (informal)

anchor.snippet: It is commutative , meaning that the order of the numbers being added does not matter

annotations.note: Encoded as the `AddCommSemigroup.add_comm` field (class declared at Defs.lean:251).

annotations.label: Associativity of addition (informal)

anchor.snippet: It is also associative , meaning that when one adds more than two numbers

annotations.note: Encoded as the `AddSemigroup.add_assoc` field (class declared at Defs.lean:182).

annotations.label: Adding 1 is counting / successor

anchor.snippet: Repeated addition of 1 is the same as counting

annotations.note: `Nat.succ_eq_add_one` states `n.succ = n + 1` (inherited from core).

annotations.label: Addition of 0 leaves number unchanged

anchor.snippet: Addition of 0 does not change a number.

annotations.note: `AddZeroClass.add_zero` (class at Defs.lean:384) is the identity axiom for additive monoids.

annotations.label: Plus sign and equals sign notation

anchor.snippet: Addition is written using the plus sign

annotations.note: The `+` notation is the standard `HAdd.hAdd` infix in Lean/Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Addition is written using the plus sign

annotations.label: Mixed number notation

anchor.snippet: a whole number followed immediately by a fraction indicates the sum of the two, called a mixed number

annotations.note: Mixed-number notation is a typographic convention not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: a whole number followed immediately by a fraction indicates the sum of the two, called a mixed number

annotations.label: Terms / addends / summands

anchor.snippet: The numbers or the objects to be added in general addition are collectively referred to as the terms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not name the arguments of `+` as addends/terms.

annotations.label: Augend

anchor.snippet: Some authors call the first addend the augend

annotations.note: Archaic English terminology not used in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Summation

anchor.snippet: An arbitrary number of addition operations is called a summation

annotations.note: Iterated finite sums are `Finset.sum`, written `∑ i ∈ s, f i`.

anchors.snippet: An arbitrary number of addition operations is called a summation

annotations.label: Series (infinite summation)

anchor.snippet: An infinite summation is a delicate procedure known as a series

annotations.note: Infinite sums are `tsum` (notation `∑' i, f i`) in topological add monoids.

anchors.snippet: An infinite summation is a delicate procedure known as a series

annotations.label: Capital sigma notation

anchor.snippet: it can be expressed through capital sigma notation

annotations.note: Mathlib provides the `∑ i ∈ s, f i` capital-sigma notation via `Finset.sum`.

anchors.snippet: it can be expressed through capital sigma notation

annotations.label: Addition as combining sets

anchor.snippet: When two or more disjoint collections are combined into a single collection, the number of objects in the single collection is the sum of the numbers of objects in the original collections.

annotations.note: `Cardinal.add_def : #α + #β = #(α ⊕ β)` (Defs.lean:249) formalizes disjoint-union cardinality.

annotations.label: Addition as extending a length

anchor.snippet: When an original length is extended by a given amount, the final length is the sum of the original length and the length of the extension.

annotations.note: Informal geometric interpretation; not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Unary addition operation

anchor.snippet: applying the unary operation

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the binary `Add` class; one-sided addition corresponds to left/right add classes but is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Commutative law of addition

anchor.snippet: Addition is commutative , meaning that one can change the order of the terms in a sum, but still get the same result.

annotations.note: `AddCommSemigroup.add_comm : a + b = b + a`.

anchors.snippet: Addition is commutative , meaning that one can change the order of the terms in a sum, but still get the same result.

annotations.label: Associative law of addition

anchor.snippet: Addition is associative , which means that when three or more numbers are added together, the order of operations does not change the result.

annotations.note: `AddSemigroup.add_assoc : a + b + c = a + (b + c)`.

anchors.snippet: Addition is associative , which means that when three or more numbers are added together, the order of operations does not change the result.

annotations.label: Additive identity (zero)

anchor.snippet: Adding zero to any number does not change the number.

annotations.note: `AddZeroClass.add_zero` together with `zero_add` give zero as additive identity.

anchors.snippet: Adding zero to any number does not change the number.

annotations.label: Successor and addition as iterated succession

anchor.snippet: Within the context of integers, addition of one also plays a special role

annotations.note: `nsmul_succ : nsmul (n+1) x = nsmul n x + x` (Defs.lean:648) formalizes iterated addition of 1/successor.

annotations.label: Successor example: 6 + 2 = 8

anchor.snippet: For example, 6 + 2 is 8, because 8 is the successor of 7

annotations.note: Numeric identity provable by `decide`/`rfl`; underlying lemma is `Nat.succ_eq_add_one`.

annotations.label: Addition requires common units

anchor.snippet: To numerically add physical quantities with units , they must be expressed with common units.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of physical units arithmetic.

annotations.label: Milliliter addition

anchor.snippet: adding 50 milliliters to 150 milliliters gives 200 milliliters

annotations.note: Physical-units example outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Feet plus inches

anchor.snippet: if a measure of 5 feet is extended by 2 inches, the sum is 62 inches

annotations.note: Physical-units example outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Carry example 59 + 27

anchor.snippet: the ones in the addition of 59 + 27 is 9 + 7 = 16, and the digit 1 is the carry

annotations.note: Pedagogical illustration of the carry algorithm; no Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: Standard algorithm for multidigit addition

anchor.snippet: The standard algorithm for adding multidigit numbers is to align the addends vertically

annotations.note: Mathlib uses semantic addition on `Nat`, not the schoolbook column-addition algorithm.

annotations.label: Addition of decimal fractions

anchor.snippet: Decimal fractions can be added by a simple modification of the above process.

annotations.note: Mathlib does not separately formalize a decimal-string addition algorithm.

annotations.label: Decimal addition 45.1 + 4.34

anchor.snippet: As an example, 45.1 + 4.34 can be solved as follows

annotations.note: Pedagogical example without a Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Addition in scientific notation

anchor.snippet: To add numbers in scientific notation, they should be expressed with the same exponent

annotations.note: No scientific-notation arithmetic procedure is encoded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary addition with carrying

anchor.snippet: Adding two single-digit binary numbers is relatively simple, using a form of carrying

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Nat`/`Num` addition is defined semantically rather than as a binary-carry algorithm.

anchors.snippet: Adding two single-digit binary numbers is relatively simple, using a form of carrying

annotations.label: Carrying in positional bases

anchor.snippet: This is known as carrying

annotations.note: Positional-carry as an abstract concept is not formalized.

annotations.label: Floating-point addition is non-associative

anchor.snippet: This makes floating-point addition non-associative in general.

annotations.note: Mathlib does not model IEEE floating-point arithmetic; non-associativity is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Addition by cardinality of disjoint union

anchor.snippet: Take two disjoint sets

annotations.note: `Cardinal.add_def : #α + #β = #(α ⊕ β)` realizes the disjoint-union definition.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of addition

anchor.snippet: The other popular definition is recursive

annotations.note: `Nat.add` is defined by primitive recursion in core Lean and reused in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dedekind's proof of associativity/commutativity

anchor.snippet: He proved the associative and commutative properties, among others, through mathematical induction

annotations.note: `Nat.add_assoc` and `Nat.add_comm` are proved by induction in core/Mathlib.

annotations.label: Addition of integers by cases

anchor.snippet: The corresponding definition of addition must proceed by cases

annotations.note: `Int.add` is defined by case analysis on the sign-magnitude representation (in core, reused by Mathlib).

annotations.label: Integer addition: −6 + 4 = −2

anchor.snippet: As an example, −6 + 4 = −2

annotations.note: A specific numeric instance of `Int.add`, provable by `decide`/`rfl`.

annotations.label: Integers as equivalence classes of pairs

anchor.snippet: one can define formally the integers as the equivalence classes of pairs of natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib uses Lean's inductive `Int` rather than the Grothendieck-pair quotient construction.

annotations.label: Component-wise addition of integer pairs

anchor.snippet: The addition of ordered pairs is done component-wise

annotations.note: General `Prod` componentwise addition exists; the specific ℕ×ℕ Grothendieck construction for ℤ is not used.

anchors.snippet: The addition of ordered pairs is done component-wise

annotations.label: Addition of rational numbers

anchor.snippet: Addition of rational numbers involves the fractions

annotations.note: `Rat.add` defines rational addition using common-denominator formulas (in core, reused by Mathlib).

anchors.snippet: Addition of rational numbers involves the fractions

annotations.label: Addition of fractions with same denominator

anchor.snippet: Addition of fractions is much simpler when the denominators are the same

annotations.note: `add_div : (a + b)/c = a/c + b/c` (Field/Basic.lean:36) gives the equal-denominator sum; `div_add_div_same` exists only on ENNReal.

anchors.snippet: Addition of fractions is much simpler when the denominators are the same

annotations.label: Commutativity and associativity of rational addition

anchor.snippet: The commutativity and associativity of rational addition are easy consequences of the laws of integer arithmetic.

annotations.note: ℚ is a `CommRing`/`Field` instance, giving `add_comm` and `add_assoc`.

annotations.label: Addition of Dedekind cuts

anchor.snippet: The sum of real numbers a and b is defined element by element

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs ℝ via Cauchy sequences, not Dedekind cuts.

anchors.snippet: The sum of real numbers a and b is defined element by element

annotations.label: Addition of Cauchy sequences

anchor.snippet: A real number is essentially defined to be the limit of a Cauchy sequence of rationals

annotations.note: `Real.add` (irreducible_def at Basic.lean:79) implements pointwise CauSeq addition on ℝ.

anchors.snippet: A real number is essentially defined to be the limit of a Cauchy sequence of rationals

annotations.label: Additive inverses on the reals

anchor.snippet: Probably the trickiest part of this construction pertaining to addition is the definition of additive inverses.

annotations.note: Additive inverses on ℝ are provided by the `Neg` instance through the `AddGroup` structure on `Real`.

annotations.label: Addition of complex numbers

anchor.snippet: Complex numbers are added by adding the real and imaginary parts of the summands.

annotations.note: `Complex.add_re`/`Complex.add_im` (Basic.lean:169,173) express componentwise addition on ℂ.

annotations.label: Parallelogram interpretation of complex addition

anchor.snippet: the sum of two complex numbers A and B , interpreted as points of the complex plane, is the point X obtained by building a parallelogram

annotations.note: The geometric parallelogram-law interpretation is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Abelian group addition

anchor.snippet: In the special case where the order does not matter, the composition operator is sometimes called addition.

annotations.note: `AddCommGroup` (Defs.lean:1275) is Mathlib's class for abelian groups written additively.

annotations.label: Vector addition

anchor.snippet: The sum of two vectors is obtained by adding their individual coordinates

annotations.note: Componentwise addition on function/coordinate spaces is `Pi.instAdd` (i.e. `(f+g) i = f i + g i`).

anchors.snippet: The sum of two vectors is obtained by adding their individual coordinates

annotations.label: Matrix addition

anchor.snippet: Matrix addition is defined for two matrices of the same dimensions.

annotations.note: `Matrix m n α` inherits an entrywise `Add` instance via `Pi`.

anchors.snippet: Matrix addition is defined for two matrices of the same dimensions.

annotations.label: Modular arithmetic addition

anchor.snippet: In modular arithmetic , the set of available numbers is restricted to a finite subset of the integers

annotations.note: `ZMod n` is Mathlib's ring of integers mod n with its `Add` instance.

annotations.label: Integers modulo 2 as XOR

anchor.snippet: The set of integers modulo 2 has just two elements

annotations.note: `ZMod 2` realizes ℤ/2 with addition equal to XOR; the XOR identification itself is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Angle addition modulo 2π

anchor.snippet: where the sum of two angle measures is often taken to be their sum as real numbers modulo 2π

annotations.note: `Real.Angle` (Angle.lean:31) is ℝ/(2π ℤ) with its additive group structure.

annotations.label: Addition in abstract algebra

anchor.snippet: The general theory of abstract algebra allows an "addition" operation to be any associative and commutative operation on a set.

annotations.note: `AddCommSemigroup` (Defs.lean:251) captures arbitrary associative+commutative addition operations.

annotations.label: Linear combinations

anchor.snippet: Linear combinations combine multiplication and summation

annotations.note: `Finsupp.linearCombination` (LinearCombination.lean:54) constructs general linear combinations as a linear map.

annotations.label: Ordinal and cardinal addition

anchor.snippet: A far-reaching generalization of the addition of natural numbers is the addition of ordinal numbers and cardinal numbers in set theory.

annotations.note: Both `Ordinal` and `Cardinal` carry `Add` instances in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-commutativity of ordinal addition

anchor.snippet: Unlike most addition operations, the addition of ordinal numbers is not commutative.

annotations.note: `Ordinal.one_add_omega0 : 1 + ω = ω` (Arithmetic.lean:1084) gives an explicit witness of non-commutativity, but no named `¬ add_comm` lemma exists.

annotations.label: Commutativity of cardinal addition

anchor.snippet: Addition of cardinal numbers, however, is a commutative operation closely related to the disjoint union operation.

annotations.note: `Cardinal.commSemiring : CommSemiring Cardinal` (Order.lean:218) yields `add_comm` for cardinal sums.

annotations.label: Coproduct as generalization of addition

anchor.snippet: In category theory , disjoint union is seen as a particular case of the coproduct operation

annotations.note: Binary coproducts `X ⨿ Y` are defined in `CategoryTheory.Limits` (BinaryProducts.lean has `coprod.desc`, `coprod.map`, etc.).

annotations.label: Subtraction as addition of inverse

anchor.snippet: Subtraction can be thought of as a kind of addition

annotations.note: `SubNegMonoid.sub_eq_add_neg : a - b = a + -b` (Defs.lean:1006).

annotations.label: Multiplication as repeated addition

anchor.snippet: Multiplication can be thought of as repeated addition .

annotations.note: `n • x = x + x + … + x` (n times) is encoded by `nsmul_succ` on additive monoids.

annotations.label: Exponential exchanges addition and multiplication

anchor.snippet: In the real and complex numbers, addition and multiplication can be interchanged by the exponential function

annotations.note: `Real.exp_add` and `Complex.exp_add` give `exp (x+y) = exp x * exp y` (used pervasively, e.g. SpecialFunctions/Exp.lean:42).

anchors.snippet: In the real and complex numbers, addition and multiplication can be interchanged by the exponential function

annotations.label: Distributivity defines a ring

anchor.snippet: multiplication operations always distribute over addition; this requirement is formalized in the definition of a ring

annotations.note: `Distrib` (Ring/Defs.lean:64) builds `left_distrib`/`right_distrib` into the ring axioms.

annotations.label: Right distributivity of division over addition

anchor.snippet: division is right distributive over addition

annotations.note: `add_div : (a + b) / c = a/c + b/c` in any division ring (Field/Basic.lean:36).

annotations.label: Division not left distributive

anchor.snippet: However, division is not left distributive over addition

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named lemma asserting failure of `c/(a+b) = c/a + c/b`.

annotations.label: Max approximates sum for different magnitudes

anchor.snippet: if two nonnegative numbers

annotations.note: Heuristic statement, not a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Cardinal sum equals greater of two infinite cardinals

anchor.snippet: is an infinite cardinal number , their cardinal sum is exactly equal to the greater of the two

annotations.note: `Cardinal.add_eq_max : ℵ₀ ≤ a → a + b = max a b` (Arithmetic.lean:233).

annotations.label: Addition distributes over max

anchor.snippet: addition distributes over "max" in the same way that multiplication distributes over addition

annotations.note: `max_add_add_left` / `max_add_add_right` give `a + max b c = max (a+b) (a+c)` in ordered (cancel) add monoids.

anchors.snippet: addition distributes over "max" in the same way that multiplication distributes over addition

annotations.label: Tropical addition / multiplication

anchor.snippet: in tropical geometry one replaces multiplication with addition and addition with maximization

annotations.note: `Tropical α` (Basic.lean:58) defines the tropical semiring with `min` as addition and `+` as multiplication.

anchors.snippet: in tropical geometry one replaces multiplication with addition and addition with maximization

annotations.label: Tropical addition via logarithm limit

anchor.snippet: tropical addition is approximately related to regular addition through the logarithm

annotations.note: The log-sum-exp/Maslov-dequantization tropicalization limit is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: tropical addition is approximately related to regular addition through the logarithm

annotations.label: Convolution as addition of random variables

anchor.snippet: Convolution is used to add two independent random variables defined by distribution functions .

annotations.note: `IndepFun` (Independence/Basic.lean:144) defines independence; the law of `X+Y` as convolution of laws is encoded via `Measure.conv` / pushforward but not as a single named lemma here.
